findings.title: SQL Injection via string formatting in query  [+2 related]
findings.description: High-fidelity incident cluster: 3 findings across 3 rule(s) [registry/django/sqli/cursor-execute, registry/fastapi/sqli/raw-sql-execute, registry/flask/sqli/string-format-query] and 1 CWE(s) [CWE-89]. Severity distribution: {'critical': 3}.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries; never format SQL with user data
findings.explanation:
### SQL Injection (CWE-89)
**What it is:**
SQL Injection occurs when user input is concatenated directly into a database query string. This allows an attacker to manipulate the query's logic, potentially bypassing authentication, wiping the database, or extracting sensitive tables.

**Why the fix works:**
By using *parameterized queries* (e.g., `(?,)` in SQLite or `%s` in psycopg2), the database driver treats the user input strictly as compiled literal data, not as executable code. Even if an attacker enters `' OR 1=1 --`, it will just be treated as a weirdly named string.

rule.title: SQL Injection via string formatting in query  [+2 related]
rule.summary: Flags user-influenced values flowing into SQL text without parameterization.
rule.remediation: Use parameterized queries or ORM bind parameters instead of string interpolation.

findings.title: Unsafe Deserialization via pickle in Django view  [+1 related]
findings.description: High-fidelity incident cluster: 2 findings across 2 rule(s) [registry/django/deserialization/pickle-in-view, registry/python/supply-chain/pickle-load-network] and 1 CWE(s) [CWE-502]. Severity distribution: {'critical': 2}.
findings.suggestion: Use JSON or Django's built-in serializers instead of pickle
findings.explanation:
### Unsafe Deserialization (CWE-502)
**What it is:**
Deserialization of untrusted data (like `pickle.loads` or `yaml.load`) is highly dangerous because these formats can instantiate arbitrary Python objects. A crafted payload can trigger `__reduce__` methods to execute OS commands instantly upon loading.

**Why the fix works:**
Switching to a pure-data serialization format like JSON (`json.loads`) ensures that only primitive dictionaries and lists are created, effectively eliminating the risk of arbitrary code execution.

rule.title: Unsafe Deserialization via pickle in Django view  [+1 related]
rule.summary: Flags use of deserializers capable of instantiating attacker-controlled objects.
rule.remediation: Prefer pure-data formats like JSON, or verify integrity before deserializing unsafe formats.

findings.title: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
findings.description: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
findings.suggestion: Resolve and validate path with os.path.realpath() against a safe base directory
findings.explanation:
### Path Traversal (CWE-22)
**What it is:**
Path traversal occurs when user-supplied input is used to construct a file path without sanitization. An attacker can use sequences like `../../etc/passwd` to read or write files outside the intended directory.

**Why the fix works:**
Using `pathlib.Path.resolve()` to get the absolute real path of the requested file, then asserting that it starts with the expected base directory (`str(resolved).startswith(str(safe_root))`), prevents any traversal regardless of how `..` sequences are encoded.

rule.title: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
rule.summary: Detects untrusted path data reaching filesystem reads, writes, or joins without confinement.
rule.remediation: Resolve against a safe base directory and reject any path that escapes it.

findings.title: CWE-798: Hardcoded API key at line 231
findings.description: A API key is hardcoded in source code at L231. This is visible in version control. Rotate this credential immediately.
findings.suggestion: Use environment variables or a secrets manager (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, .env excluded from git).
findings.explanation:
### Hard-coded Credentials (CWE-798)
**What it is:**
Embedding passwords, API keys, or secrets directly in source code means anyone with read access to the repository—including CI logs, forks, and git history—can extract and misuse them.

**Why the fix works:**
Credentials should be loaded at runtime from environment variables (`os.environ["SECRET_KEY"]`) or a secrets manager. This keeps secrets out of the codebase entirely and allows rotation without a code change.

rule.title: Python hardcoded credential
rule.summary: Flags likely credentials, API keys, or signing secrets committed directly to source.
rule.remediation: Move secrets to environment variables or a secrets manager and rotate any exposed values.

findings.title: SQL Injection via string formatting in query  [+2 related]
findings.description: High-fidelity incident cluster: 3 findings across 3 rule(s) [registry/django/sqli/cursor-execute, registry/fastapi/sqli/raw-sql-execute, registry/flask/sqli/string-format-query] and 1 CWE(s) [CWE-89]. Severity distribution: {'critical': 3}.
findings.suggestion: Use parameterized queries; never format SQL with user data
findings.explanation:
### SQL Injection (CWE-89)
**What it is:**
SQL Injection occurs when user input is concatenated directly into a database query string. This allows an attacker to manipulate the query's logic, potentially bypassing authentication, wiping the database, or extracting sensitive tables.

**Why the fix works:**
By using *parameterized queries* (e.g., `(?,)` in SQLite or `%s` in psycopg2), the database driver treats the user input strictly as compiled literal data, not as executable code. Even if an attacker enters `' OR 1=1 --`, it will just be treated as a weirdly named string.

rule.title: SQL Injection via string formatting in query  [+2 related]
rule.summary: Flags user-influenced values flowing into SQL text without parameterization.
rule.remediation: Use parameterized queries or ORM bind parameters instead of string interpolation.

findings.title: Command Injection via subprocess.getstatusoutput
findings.description: Command Injection via subprocess.getstatusoutput
findings.suggestion: Use subprocess.run with shell=False and a list of arguments
findings.explanation:
### OS Command Injection (CWE-78)
**What it is:**
Command Injection happens when an application passes unsafe user-supplied data to a system shell. This can lead to arbitrary command execution (e.g., `; rm -rf /` appended to an image name).

**Why the fix works:**
By turning `shell=True` to `shell=False` and passing arguments as a list (e.g., `['ls', '-l', user_dir]`), the operating system executes the binary directly without passing the string through a shell interpreter (`sh` or `cmd.exe`). This neutralizes shell metacharacters like `;`, `&&`, and `|`.

rule.title: Command Injection via subprocess.getstatusoutput
rule.summary: Finds user-influenced values flowing into shell execution or dynamic command sinks.
rule.remediation: Use argument arrays, avoid shell expansion, and validate commands against a strict allowlist.

findings.title: Insecure Deserialization via marshal.loads with user data
findings.description: Insecure Deserialization via marshal.loads with user data
findings.suggestion: marshal is not safe for untrusted data; use JSON or another safe serialization format
findings.explanation:
### Unsafe Deserialization (CWE-502)
**What it is:**
Deserialization of untrusted data (like `pickle.loads` or `yaml.load`) is highly dangerous because these formats can instantiate arbitrary Python objects. A crafted payload can trigger `__reduce__` methods to execute OS commands instantly upon loading.

**Why the fix works:**
Switching to a pure-data serialization format like JSON (`json.loads`) ensures that only primitive dictionaries and lists are created, effectively eliminating the risk of arbitrary code execution.

rule.title: Insecure Deserialization via marshal.loads with user data
rule.summary: Flags use of deserializers capable of instantiating attacker-controlled objects.
rule.remediation: Prefer pure-data formats like JSON, or verify integrity before deserializing unsafe formats.

findings.title: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
findings.description: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
findings.suggestion: Resolve and validate path with os.path.realpath() against a safe base directory
findings.explanation:
### Path Traversal (CWE-22)
**What it is:**
Path traversal occurs when user-supplied input is used to construct a file path without sanitization. An attacker can use sequences like `../../etc/passwd` to read or write files outside the intended directory.

**Why the fix works:**
Using `pathlib.Path.resolve()` to get the absolute real path of the requested file, then asserting that it starts with the expected base directory (`str(resolved).startswith(str(safe_root))`), prevents any traversal regardless of how `..` sequences are encoded.

rule.title: Path Traversal via open() with unsanitized user input
rule.summary: Detects untrusted path data reaching filesystem reads, writes, or joins without confinement.
rule.remediation: Resolve against a safe base directory and reject any path that escapes it.

findings.title: CWE-639: Possible IDOR in `test_sqlalchemy_filter_owner_check_no_finding()` — authenticated object access without ownership check
findings.description: `test_sqlalchemy_filter_owner_check_no_finding()` performs authentication and direct object access but lacks an ownership filter (owner=request.user). Attackers may access other users' objects by enumerating IDs.
findings.suggestion: Filter queried objects by the current user: `obj = Model.objects.get(pk=pk, owner=request.user)`.
findings.explanation:
### Insecure Direct Object Reference / IDOR (CWE-639)
**What it is:**
IDOR occurs when a route retrieves or modifies a resource using an ID supplied by the caller without verifying that the caller actually owns or is authorized to access that resource. Any authenticated user can access any other user's data by guessing or incrementing IDs.

**Why the fix works:**
Every database query on a resource that belongs to a user must include an ownership check in the WHERE clause: `WHERE id = ? AND owner_id = current_user.id`. This makes it impossible to retrieve records belonging to a different user even with a valid session.

rule.title: CWE-639: Possible IDOR in `test_sqlalchemy_filter_owner_check_no_finding()` — authenticated object access without ownership check
rule.summary: Finds resource fetches by attacker-controlled IDs without a matching owner or tenant restriction.
rule.remediation: Scope queries by both resource identifier and verified owner or tenant identity.
known_limitations: Heuristic route analysis does not prove exploitability when ownership is enforced in external services.

findings.title: Open Redirect via HttpResponseRedirect
findings.description: Open Redirect via HttpResponseRedirect
findings.suggestion: Validate redirect URL against a safe allowlist or use url_has_allowed_host_and_scheme()
findings.explanation:
### Open Redirect (CWE-601)
**What it is:**
When a `redirect()` or `Location` header is built from user-controlled input without validation, attackers can craft URLs like `/logout?next=https://evil.com` to redirect victims to phishing pages after a legitimate flow.

**Why the fix works:**
Validate the `next` or `redirect_to` parameter against an allowlist of safe paths or same-origin URLs before redirecting. The simplest check: ensure the redirect target is a relative path that starts with `/` and does not start with `//`.

rule.title: Open Redirect via HttpResponseRedirect
rule.summary: Flags redirects whose destination comes from user input without host or path validation.
rule.remediation: Allow only relative paths or a strict allowlist of trusted redirect hosts.
